07.04.25 08:04
Subject: Project members (external) lose access to SharePoint
From: Contact Person 07
Good morning

/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025

08.04.25 08:45

08.04.25 10:15

10.04.25 08:55
Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 14
In-depth analysis
10.04.25 09:40
Azure AD Connect reviewed.
Observation:
Group “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” exists both On-Prem and in Cloud.

Cloud version was originally created manually.
Result:
• External guests exist only in Cloud group
• SharePoint references the synchronized On-Prem group
Same name, but different Object IDs.
10.04.25 11:25

Cloud group:
→ Contains external guests
Synchronized On-Prem group:
→ Contains only internal users
Since SharePoint evaluates by Object ID, not by name, external users lose access once synchronization takes effect.
11.04.25 08:30

• On-Prem group re-synchronized
• Cloud group overwritten
• External users reproducibly lose access
Root Cause
Two groups existed with identical name:
COMPLIANCE_TEAM
1. Cloud-native group
2. Synchronized On-Prem group
Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated.
SharePoint referenced the synchronized version.
External guests were never managed On-Prem and therefore were not included in the synchronized group.

11.04.25 – 14.04.25
• Consolidation of group structure

17.04.25 15:18 – Ticket closure

The root cause was the parallel existence of two groups with identical names but different origins (Cloud vs. On-Prem synchronized).

# Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard (D365) — Customer Company 11

An approved operational and architecture standard (Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard V1.0) defines the design, security configuration and operational procedures for the Financial Reporting stack used in Customer Company 11's D365 Finance & Operations environments (UAT and PROD). The standard names a small set of high-impact components and dependencies: the Financial Reporting Data Mart (aggregated SQL store), the SQL backend that hosts metadata, an IIS/TLS layer that enforces cipher suites, service accounts used by reporting components, and operational actions such as Data Mart resets and decision-tree guided troubleshooting. The decision-tree/use-cases section maps observable symptoms (for example, reports showing authors as “unknown”) to concrete checks (service account connectivity, TLS/cipher-suite compatibility, SQL connectivity, AOS service, IIS configuration) and prescribes synchronous, environment-consistent changes (for cipher suites, AOS restarts, and Data Mart resets) to avoid broken flows across reporting components. The document is specifically authored for Customer Company 11 and is the authoritative reference tying troubleshooting, configuration management and recovery actions together.

## Standard document is the central governance artifact for reporting infrastructure

The Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard V1.0 is the controlling architecture and operational standard for the Financial Reporting components in Customer Company 11's D365 environment and explicitly applies to UAT and PROD environments, making it the authoritative source for configuration and runbook behavior [Data: Entities (476); Relationships (778)].

Because the decision-tree and use-case sections are embedded in the same document, the standard both prescribes expected runtime configuration (TLS/cipher-suite rules, service-account behavior) and enumerates the troubleshooting workflow that engineers must follow when incidents occur, tying governance to operations [Data: Entities (476, 484); Relationships (784)].

## Financial Reporting Data Mart is a single, critical dependency for report availability and correctness

The Financial Reporting Data Mart is described as an aggregated SQL-based store relied upon by the Report Designer and Financial Reporting Service; its consistency is essential to correct report results and availability, which makes it a primary dependency in incident investigations [Data: Entities (478); Relationships (792)].

Operational guidance in the standard endorses a Data Mart reset as a remediation when data inconsistency is isolated to a single environment, indicating that the Data Mart is both a frequent root-cause locus and a recoverable subsystem when handled according to the runbook [Data: Entities (489); Relationships (795)].

## SQL backend is the metadata authority and first-line troubleshooting target

Report metadata and authorship information are stored and retrieved via the SQL backend/Data Mart; therefore SQL connectivity and database health directly affect metadata visibility and report rendering [Data: Entities (482, 485); Relationships (807)].

The decision-tree explicitly includes SQL connection checks as a primary step when reports or data are unavailable, making SQL health an early and repeatable check in incident triage workflows [Data: Entities (484, 482); Relationships (806)].

## IIS/TLS layer and cipher-suite configuration are high-risk failure points for authentication and metadata issues

An IIS-based TLS termination layer enforces TLS and cipher-suite settings for the reporting infrastructure; the standard highlights that misconfiguration here can block or degrade report access and functionality by breaking TLS negotiation between clients and backend components [Data: Entities (479, 486); Relationships (800, 802)].

The decision tree links TLS/cipher-suite incompatibility to observable symptoms such as report authors shown as “unknown,” indicating that TLS failures can manifest as metadata/authentication failures rather than obvious transport-level errors, and therefore must be included in runbooks and monitoring thresholds [Data: Relationships (804); Entities (491, 486)].

## Service account connectivity and configuration are primary causes of missing author metadata

The standard calls out service accounts as the identities used by reporting components to access the Data Mart and metadata; when report authors appear as “unknown,” the runbook specifically prescribes checking service account connectivity and permissions as a primary diagnostic action [Data: Entities (481, 491); Relationships (792, 805)].

This linkage makes service-account lifecycle controls (password/credential rotation, consistent configuration across environments) and monitoring of account authentication health operational priorities to avoid metadata loss and degraded author attribution [Data: Entities (481); Relationships (805)].

## Decision tree maps symptoms to concrete component checks and environment-consistent change rules

The document section containing decision trees and use cases maps specific symptoms to component checks (Data Mart reset, SQL connection, AOS service, IIS configuration, cipher-suite/TLS checks), providing an actionable troubleshooting workflow that ties observable issues to discrete remediation steps [Data: Entities (484); Relationships (784, 806)].

The standard further prescribes operational principles such as synchronizing cipher-suite changes across environments and performing AOS restarts after certain adjustments, which reduce the risk of asymmetric changes causing environment-specific failures; these procedural constraints should be enforced by change control and deployment automation [Data: Entities (476); Relationships (784)].

## Recurring failure mode pattern: TLS/cipher mismatches and service-account authentication produce metadata anomalies

The provided artifacts explicitly link TLS/cipher-suite incompatibility to both transport-level failures and the symptom of unknown report authors, and they separately identify service-account authentication problems as a cause of the same symptom, demonstrating a common failure-mode cluster where authentication/crypto misconfiguration and identity issues produce indistinct metadata anomalies [Data: Relationships (804, 805); Entities (486, 481, 491)].

Because multiple components (IIS/TLS layer, SQL backend/Data Mart, service accounts) can manifest the same operational symptom, the decision-tree's ordered checks (service accounts, TLS, SQL, Data Mart reset) should be codified into runbooks and automated diagnostics to avoid repeated firefighting and to direct engineers to the most-likely root cause quickly [Data: Entities (484, 478, 482); Relationships (792, 806, 795)].

## Operational recovery guidance exists but relies on environment consistency and synchronous changes

The standard contains explicit instructions that certain configuration changes (for example, allowed cipher suites) be synchronized across environments and that AOS restarts and Data Mart resets be used in a controlled manner; this indicates that successful recovery depends on disciplined change management and environment parity [Data: Entities (476, 486, 489); Relationships (784, 795)].

Absent automated enforcement of environment parity and pre-change compatibility checks, there is risk that asynchronous or partial rollouts (e.g., different cipher suites in UAT vs PROD) will create intermittent, environment-specific failures; the document's direction therefore implies the need for gating changes through the standard's decision-tree and CI/CD controls [Data: Entities (476, 486); Relationships (784)].

## Controls and monitoring gaps implied by the document should be prioritized for remediation

The artifact set highlights the criticality of TLS/cipher configuration, SQL/Data Mart health, and service-account authentication but does not itself include explicit monitoring/alert thresholds or automated remediation steps in the extracted records; given these components' operational impact, establishing targeted alerts for TLS negotiation failures, SQL connectivity errors, service-account authentication failures, and Data Mart consistency checks should be a near-term priority [Data: Entities (479, 482, 481, 478); Relationships (800, 806, 792, 795)].

Formalizing these checks into runbooks and integrating them with the decision-tree/use-cases in the standard (for example, automated pre-flight checks before cipher-suite changes and automated Data Mart consistency verification) will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for the recurring failure modes documented here [Data: Entities (484, 476); Relationships (784)].

Owner: Employee 31
Origin: Email
Type: Incident
Created on: 07.04.25 08:12
Closure
Closure date: 17.04.25 15:18
Closure text: Root cause identified (Azure AD Connect synchronization error affecting group attributes). Cleanup and full sync performed. Access stable.
Activities
07.04.25 08:04
Subject: Project members (external) lose access to SharePoint
From: Contact Person 07
Good morning
Several external project members report that they no longer have access to the folder
/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025
Internal employees do not appear to have issues.
Please review.
Kind regards
Contact Person 07
07.04.25 08:12
Ticket created
Assigned to: Employee 31
Status: In Progress
Notes
07.04.25 09:05
Helpdesk asks:
• Does it affect only external users?
• What error message appears?
• Since when exactly?
• Were groups or permissions adjusted?
07.04.25 10:20
Customer responds:
Error message:
Access denied
One user could access yesterday.
Today no longer.
No deliberate changes made.
07.04.25 11:10
Helpdesk checks SharePoint:
• Folder permissions correct
• Group “SP_Compliance_Main” contains “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”
• External users are members of “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”
Comparison internal vs external users:
No visible difference.
08.04.25 08:45
Customer adds:
One external user was newly added and immediately had access.
Another who has been in the project for months loses access sporadically.
08.04.25 10:15
Helpdesk tests:
• Remove + re-add to group
→ Access temporarily restored
After approx. 1 hour, no access again.
08.04.25 14:30
Hypothesis:
Token / cache / Conditional Access
Test:
User fully logs out → logs in again
→ Access remains denied
Hypothesis rejected.
09.04.25 09:12
Helpdesk asks:
Are external users created as “Guest” or “Member”?
09.04.25 10:05
Customer:
All externals are created as Guest.
Some were recently synchronized from On-Prem AD to Cloud.
09.04.25 11:40
First observation:
For two affected users in Entra ID portal, attribute
“source” = Windows Server AD
For functioning external users:
“source” = Azure AD
Correlation unclear.
09.04.25 14:20
Ticket escalated for in-depth analysis.
Assigned Group changed to: BU Infrastructure – Identity
10.04.25 08:55
Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 14
In-depth analysis
10.04.25 09:40
Azure AD Connect reviewed.
Observation:
Group “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” exists both On-Prem and in Cloud.
On-Prem version is synchronized.
Cloud version was originally created manually.
Result:
• External guests exist only in Cloud group
• SharePoint references the synchronized On-Prem group
Same name, but different Object IDs.
10.04.25 11:25
Review of group members via Graph:
Cloud group:
→ Contains external guests
Synchronized On-Prem group:
→ Contains only internal users
Since SharePoint evaluates by Object ID, not by name, external users lose access once synchronization takes effect.
11.04.25 08:30
Reproduction in test:
• On-Prem group re-synchronized
• Cloud group overwritten
• External users reproducibly lose access
Root Cause
Two groups existed with identical name:
COMPLIANCE_TEAM
1. Cloud-native group
2. Synchronized On-Prem group
Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated.
SharePoint referenced the synchronized version.
External guests were never managed On-Prem and therefore were not included in the synchronized group.
Actions
11.04.25 – 14.04.25
• Consolidation of group structure
• Old Cloud group removed
• On-Prem group properly cleaned
• External guests managed as Cloud-only members
• Azure AD Connect full synchronization performed
15.04.25 – Monitoring
Access remains stable
No further inconsistencies
17.04.25 15:18 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 07
The root cause was the parallel existence of two groups with identical names but different origins (Cloud vs. On-Prem synchronized).
Due to synchronization, effective group membership for external users was not correctly applied.
The group structure was consolidated and fully synchronized.
Access is now stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

02.12.25 08:55 – Phone report

02.12.25 09:20 – Initial analysis
Remote connection established with the affected user.
Printer “MFP-Basel-01” is visible.
When sending a print job, “Access denied” appears immediately.

02.12.25 09:45 – Comparison with another user
Test performed with colleague in the same office.
Printing works without restriction.
User account checked.
Membership in location group “PRT-Basel” confirmed.
02.12.25 10:30 – Further review
Comparison with “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Version 1.0”.
It specifies:
• Printer permissions are assigned exclusively via AD groups
• No individual user permissions at queue level
• NTFS and share permissions must be consistent
Review of the printer queue on the print server shows:
• AD group “PRT-Basel” has print permission

User logged in again.
Test print successful.
03.12.25 09:40 – Customer feedback
Printing works normally again.
Additional tests within the team successful.
03.12.25 10:21 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 20
The root cause was an explicit “Deny” permission on the printer queue for the affected user.
The permission structure was cleaned up according to the “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard”.
Printing is now fully functional again.

# TI-722511 — Financial Reports outage (AOS/IIS TLS cipher-suite change)

Ticket TI-722511 documents a reproducible outage where users (EMEA finance) could not open Financial Reports in both UAT and PROD; the incident was triaged with a consulting partner and resolved by activating best-practice TLS cipher suites using IISCRYPTO and restarting AOS (and IIS) servers. Key infrastructure components implicated are AOS servers and IIS servers (TLS/cipher negotiation), TLS policy/configuration and Windows security updates as potential contributors; an internal JIRA (ICO261-183) from another region reported the same error, indicating a global or multi-region manifestation that requires coordinated configuration and testing across environments. Ownership and operational actions were recorded in portal notes (contact persons and consulting company involvement), and the ticket closure explicitly links the TLS/cipher change and server restarts to the remediation outcome.

## TI-722511 is a production-impacting, multi-environment incident resolved by TLS/cipher-suite changes and server restarts

The ticket TI-722511 records reproducible errors accessing Financial Reports across UAT and PROD and documents closure text indicating that best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and AOS servers were restarted as the remediation that resolved the issue. This linkage between the ticket, the tool (IISCRYPTO), the configuration change and the server restarts is explicitly documented in the closure notes and portal history [Data: Entities (0); Entities (18,20); Relationships (9,34,36)].

Because the ticket affects both UAT and PROD and includes symptom reports from EMEA finance users (report creators appearing as "unknown" and inability to open reports), this is a production-grade operational incident rather than a single-user problem; the ticket metadata and relationships show cross-environment impact and operator action items that are relevant to architecture and runbook updates [Data: Entities (0); Relationships (1,10)].

## AOS and IIS servers are central operational dependencies for the reporting service

AOS servers broker requests from user clients (browser/Report Designer) to backend reporting services and were explicitly restarted as part of the remediation, showing they are a required operational touchpoint when TLS/cipher changes are applied. The relationships document the dependency between AOS servers, users and TLS policy changes, and record that AOS restarts were necessary after cipher adjustments [Data: Entities (19,483,487); Relationships (796,798,36)].

IIS servers are also the target of IISCRYPTO changes and therefore form the TLS termination layer that directly affects client report access; the record linking IISCRYPTO to IIS servers and to the best-practice cipher-suite configuration confirms they are part of the change domain that produced the outage [Data: Entities (26,18,20); Relationships (35,34)].

## TLS / cipher-suite configuration and Windows security updates are a plausible root-cause vector and must be part of diagnostics

The dataset explicitly identifies TLS/cipher-suite configuration as a primary decision-tree item for multi-environment report failures and calls out that Windows security updates can alter available crypto libraries or TLS behavior, impacting negotiation and compatibility [Data: Entities (480,488); Relationships (803)].

Operationally this means any change to the TLS configuration (cipher suites or minimum TLS versions) or a cumulative OS/security patch may introduce incompatibilities between client/report-designer, IIS, AOS and backend services; the incident remediation (activating best-practice ciphers and restarting servers) and the diagnostic checklist in the records both point to these items as the first-line troubleshooting steps [Data: Entities (0,20,480,488); Relationships (9,803,36)].

## The incident shows cross-regional recurrence — internal Jira ICO261-183 reports the same error

An internal JIRA ticket (ICO261-183) opened by another internal team (Mexico) reported the same error, and the portal notes for TI-722511 reference that JIRA ticket, indicating the problem was observed outside a single operating region and should be treated as a global/tenant-level issue requiring coordinated remediation and communication [Data: Entities (17); Relationships (33); Entities (0); Relationships (0)].

This cross-reference implies the root cause is likely systemic (configuration or policy across environments) rather than an isolated host-level fault, elevating the need for a deployment-wide review of TLS/cipher policies, synchronized change windows, and shared runbooks to avoid reoccurrence [Data: Entities (17,0); Relationships (33,9)].

## Consulting company acted as primary triage/coordination but operational ownership and escalation paths are fragmented

Portal no-reply entries and the ticket relationships identify a consulting organization performing triage, posting portal updates and coordinating remediation activities for TI-722511, which indicates third-party involvement in operational execution [Data: Entities (24); Relationships (16)].

While external support can accelerate remediation, the records show multiple actors (customer contact, consulting company, internal teams, and referenced JIRA) are involved; this multiplicity increases coordination overhead and suggests the need to codify explicit escalation and handoff steps (who is authorized to change TLS settings, who validates post-change testing, and who owns cross-region communication) to reduce confusion during future incidents [Data: Entities (2,24,17); Relationships (1,16,33)].

## The ticket closure documents a fix but there is limited explicit trace of pre- and post-change testing and rollback procedures

Closure text states best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and servers restarted, and this outcome is associated with resolution of the ticket, but the dataset does not include detailed pre-change compatibility testing results, a staged rollout record, or an explicit rollback plan linked to the change [Data: Entities (0,18,20); Relationships (9,34)].

For production TLS changes that can break client compatibility, the absence of documented staged testing or rollback guidance in the ticket and relationships represents an operational risk — future changes should include mandatory compatibility validation (Report Designer and browser tests across supported clients), versioned configuration snapshots, and an approved rollback procedure recorded in the change ticket before applying IISCRYPTO or similar tooling [Data: Entities (0,480,18); Relationships (9,34,35)].

## Recommended immediate actions: formalize TLS change control, add AOS/IIS restart playbook, and update monitoring/alerts

Given the documented remediation and dependencies, the highest-priority operational actions are: 1) formalize a TLS/cipher-suite change control process that enforces synchronized configuration across UAT and PROD and requires pre-change validation; 2) add a concise runbook for TLS changes that includes steps to use IISCRYPTO, how and when to restart IIS and AOS servers, and post-change verification for report access; and 3) tune monitoring/alerts to detect report designer failures, unknown report-owner symptoms, and TLS negotiation failures so teams can detect regressions quickly. These recommendations are directly derived from the incident facts that link TLS configuration, IISCRYPTO use, and server restarts to resolution [Data: Entities (0,18,20,19,26,480); Relationships (9,34,36,35,796)].

Implementing these controls will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for similar incidents and make cross-team communication and escalation more predictable.

## Governance and long-term mitigation: standardize TLS baselines, document supported client profiles, and maintain a cross-region configuration registry

The incident demonstrates the need for organizational governance around TLS baselines (approved cipher suites and minimum TLS versions) and explicit documentation of supported client versions for the Report Designer and browser clients. Records show the problem manifested across regions and environments and was tied to cipher-suite changes, reinforcing that a single source of truth for TLS configuration and supported client compatibility is required [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (803,796,36)].

A practical control is a configuration registry (or CMDB extension) that records TLS/cipher settings per environment and a pre-deployment verification checklist that gates any change; combine this with scheduled compatibility testing against a matrix of client versions to avoid breaking production users when adopting "best-practice" cryptographic settings that may remove older ciphers still required by legacy clients [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (36,796)].

## Communication and escalation runbook: define stakeholder matrix and cross-reference internal tracking systems

The records link the ticket to named customer contacts (Contact Person 7, Regina Jagsch), a consulting company, and an internal JIRA that reported the same error, indicating multiple stakeholders and tracking systems were in use during the incident [Data: Entities (2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)].

To streamline future incidents, create a short escalation runbook that maps roles (customer contact, platform-owner, consulting SPOC, security owner), primary communication channels, and which external/internal ticketing system must be updated (portal ticket and JIRA). Include templates for status updates and a requirement to escalate to platform engineering when TLS/cipher changes are proposed, ensuring a single coordinated change window and clear ownership for rollback and postmortem actions [Data: Entities (0,2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)].

10.04.25 08:55

10.04.25 11:25

→ Contains external guests
Synchronized On-Prem group:
→ Contains only internal users

• Cloud group overwritten
• External users reproducibly lose access

Two groups existed with identical name:

2. Synchronized On-Prem group
Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated.

11.04.25 – 14.04.25

+41 44 785 23 11

• Normalization of incoming numbers
• E.164 format
• Translation rules on SBC
• Voice routing policies

New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table.
Routing rule covers only the old number range.

Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range.
Test call performed.
Call correctly signaled and delivered.
Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible.
25.09.25 – Coordination with customer
Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated.
Internal documentation had not yet been updated.
26.09.25 – Adjustment
Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification.
Details not documented in ticket for security reasons.
29.09.25 – Customer feedback
All tested numbers are working again.
Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls.
Thank you for the support.
29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 22
The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range.
The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture.
Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

Helpdesk checks SharePoint:
• Folder permissions correct
• Group “SP_Compliance_Main” contains “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”
• External users are members of “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”
Comparison internal vs external users:
No visible difference.
08.04.25 08:45
Customer adds:
One external user was newly added and immediately had access.
Another who has been in the project for months loses access sporadically.
08.04.25 10:15
Helpdesk tests:
• Remove + re-add to group
→ Access temporarily restored
After approx. 1 hour, no access again.
08.04.25 14:30
Hypothesis:
Token / cache / Conditional Access
Test:

→ Access remains denied
Hypothesis rejected.
09.04.25 09:12
Helpdesk asks:
Are external users created as “Guest” or “Member”?
09.04.25 10:05
Customer:
All externals are created as Guest.
Some were recently synchronized from On-Prem AD to Cloud.
09.04.25 11:40
First observation:
For two affected users in Entra ID portal, attribute
“source” = Windows Server AD
For functioning external users:
“source” = Azure AD
Correlation unclear.
09.04.25 14:20
Ticket escalated for in-depth analysis.
Assigned Group changed to: BU Infrastructure – Identity
10.04.25 08:55
Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 14
In-depth analysis
10.04.25 09:40
Azure AD Connect reviewed.
Observation:
Group “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” exists both On-Prem and in Cloud.
On-Prem version is synchronized.
Cloud version was originally created manually.
Result:
• External guests exist only in Cloud group
• SharePoint references the synchronized On-Prem group
Same name, but different Object IDs.
10.04.25 11:25
Review of group members via Graph:
Cloud group:
→ Contains external guests
Synchronized On-Prem group:
→ Contains only internal users
Since SharePoint evaluates by Object ID, not by name, external users lose access once synchronization takes effect.
11.04.25 08:30
Reproduction in test:
• On-Prem group re-synchronized
• Cloud group overwritten
• External users reproducibly lose access
Root Cause
Two groups existed with identical name:
COMPLIANCE_TEAM
1. Cloud-native group
2. Synchronized On-Prem group
Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated.
SharePoint referenced the synchronized version.
External guests were never managed On-Prem and therefore were not included in the synchronized group.
Actions
11.04.25 – 14.04.25
• Consolidation of group structure
• Old Cloud group removed
• On-Prem group properly cleaned
• External guests managed as Cloud-only members
• Azure AD Connect full synchronization performed
15.04.25 – Monitoring
Access remains stable
No further inconsistencies
17.04.25 15:18 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 07
The root cause was the parallel existence of two groups with identical names but different origins (Cloud vs. On-Prem synchronized).
Due to synchronization, effective group membership for external users was not correctly applied.
The group structure was consolidated and fully synchronized.
Access is now stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

07.04.25 08:04
Subject: Project members (external) lose access to SharePoint
From: Contact Person 07

/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025

07.04.25 09:05

07.04.25 10:20

07.04.25 11:10

08.04.25 08:45

Another who has been in the project for months loses access sporadically.
08.04.25 10:15

08.04.25 14:30

Hypothesis rejected.

10.04.25 08:55

In-depth analysis
10.04.25 09:40

10.04.25 11:25

Synchronized On-Prem group:

Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated.
